hero.title: Your Week in Oahu, Hawaii
hero.subtitle:
A curated journey through Hawaii's most vibrant island.
Crafted for two.

bestTime.title: Best Time to Visit
bestTime.body: April through June and September through November offer ideal weather. Warm, generally drier, and with fewer crowds. Avoid late December holidays if you want more affordable rates.

getting.title: Getting There from LA
getting.body: Direct flights from LAX to HNL take about 5.5 hours. Hawaiian Airlines, Alaska Airlines, and other major carriers offer frequent nonstops. Book 6 to 8 weeks ahead for the best fares, often around $300 to $500 round trip in shoulder seasons.

waikiki.name: Waikiki
waikiki.desc: Convenient, lively, walkable: ideal for first-timers. Great beach access, big-resort amenities, and a dense restaurant and bar scene.

kailua.name: Kailua
kailua.desc: Charming small-town feel on the windward side. Local boutiques, quieter beaches, and a more residential, authentic slice of Oahu.

northShore.name: North Shore
northShore.desc: Surfer culture, food trucks, and dramatic scenery. Legendary big waves in winter, calmer swimming and snorkeling in summer.

itinerary.title: 7-Day Itinerary
itinerary.subtitle: A carefully paced week balancing beach time, local food, and authentic discovery.

days.title: Arrival in Waikiki
days.morning: Arrive at HNL, collect your rental car, and check into your Waikiki hotel. Freshen up, unpack, and take a short walk around the neighborhood to get your bearings.
days.afternoon: Walk the length of Waikiki Beach, rent a surfboard or take a lesson from a beach boy. Swim in the calm, warm waters and ease into island time.
days.evening: Watch the sunset from the sand or from the seawall near Royal Hawaiian, then head to Duke's Waikiki for dinner right on the beach. Open daily, and bar seating is usually available without a reservation.
days.tip: Duke's doesn't take reservations for bar seating. Arrive before sunset for the best chance at a table right on the sand.

days.title: Rest Day in Waikiki
days.morning: Sleep in and take your time. Pick up fresh malasadas from Leonard's Bakery or grab an acai bowl from Aloh Health Bar on Seaside Ave. No agenda today.
days.afternoon: Spend the afternoon at Waikiki Beach at your own pace. Swim, rent a paddleboard, grab a shave ice from Island Vintage Shave Ice in Waikiki, or simply find a spot in the shade. Browse the Waikiki Beach Walk shops if you feel like moving around.
days.evening: Catch the Ke Kai Catamaran sunset sail from Waikiki Beach. Check-in is at 5 PM with a 5:30 PM departure, and the sail runs 90 minutes. Drinks are complimentary. Dolphin and sea turtle sightings are common. A perfect evening on the water.
days.tip: Book the Ke Kai sail a few days ahead, especially for weekend slots. The boat departs from the beach in front of Duke's Restaurant at the Outrigger Waikiki. Check in 30 minutes before departure or you may lose your spot.

days.title: Kualoa Ranch and the East Coast
days.morning: Head to Kualoa Ranch, a famous filming location for Jurassic Park, Lost, and many others. Book a UTV Raptor tour, movie sites tour, or zipline combo for a half-day in the valley. The scenery is lush and dramatic, and popular time slots often book up well in advance.
days.afternoon: Drive south along the coast to Waimanalo Beach Park, one of Oahu's longest and less-crowded beaches. Enjoy soft sand and a Ko'olau mountain backdrop. A great spot to bodyboard or simply decompress before heading back.
days.evening: Dinner at Roy's Hawaii Kai. Roy Yamaguchi's original Hawaiian fusion restaurant with Maunalua Bay views. Open daily 4:30 PM to 9 PM (9:30 PM Fri-Sat). Happy hour 4:30 to 5:30 PM Mon-Thu. Reservations recommended. Located on the drive back to Waikiki from Waimanalo, about 20 minutes from the beach.
days.tip: Kualoa Ranch tours sell out weeks ahead, book online early. Roy's takes reservations and they go fast for weekend evenings. Hawaii Kai is about 30 minutes from Waikiki.

days.title: Diamond Head and Kakaako
days.morning: Hike Diamond Head crater at 6 AM, before the heat and crowds arrive. The 1.6-mile round trip passes through old military tunnels and ends with panoramic summit views of Waikiki and the Pacific. Book tickets online in advance ($5 per person plus $10 parking). The park opens at 6 AM. After the hike, head toward Kakaako.
days.afternoon: Explore Kakaako, Honolulu's arts and culture hub. Stop by Liliha Bakery for the legendary coco puffs. Walk the mural-lined streets, browse SALT at Our Kakaako and Ward Village for boutiques and galleries, and take in the waterfront at Kakaako Waterfront Park.
days.evening: Dinner at The Pig and The Lady in Kaimuki at Civil Beat Plaza. Chef Andrew Le's celebrated modern Vietnamese restaurant moved here from Chinatown in late 2025. The pho French dip, ahi tataki toast, and Le fried chicken wings are must-orders. Lunch Wednesday through Sunday, dinner Tuesday through Saturday from 5 PM. Closed Mondays. Reservations strongly recommended.
days.tip: Diamond Head tickets sell out, so book online before your trip. Kakaako is about 15 minutes from Waikiki by car. The Pig and The Lady is very popular and reservations can be hard to secure on short notice: book as far ahead as you can.

days.title: Hanauma Bay and North Shore
days.morning: Start at Hanauma Bay Nature Preserve for snorkeling in a protected marine sanctuary. Reservations are required and open two days in advance at 7 AM HST. They often sell out within minutes. Entry is $25 per person plus $3 parking (cash only). The bay is open Wednesday through Sunday, 6:45 AM to 4 PM with last entry at 1:30 PM. Closed Mondays and Tuesdays. Arrive early for parking. All visitors watch a mandatory reef education video before entering.
days.afternoon: Drive north to Laniakea Beach, where Hawaiian green sea turtles often come ashore to rest. Volunteers are on-site daily to educate visitors and keep everyone at a respectful distance. Then explore Haleiwa town for surf shops and murals. Stop at Giovanni's Shrimp Truck for garlic shrimp plates over rice. The original North Shore shrimp truck since 1993, open daily 10:30 AM to 5 PM at the Haleiwa location. Expect lines at peak lunch. Finish with a swim or wave watching at Waimea Bay depending on the season.
days.evening: Dinner at Haleiwa Joe's on the North Shore for fresh seafood in a relaxed, waterfront setting. Open daily from 4:30 PM Monday through Thursday and from 11 AM Friday through Sunday. No reservations taken, so arrive early to avoid a wait.
days.tip: Book Hanauma Bay reservations as soon as the window opens, two days before your visit. North Shore parking lots at Laniakea and Waimea Bay can fill by 9 AM on weekends. Arrive early and never leave valuables visible in your car.

days.title: Windward Coast and Kailua
days.morning: Drive the Pali Highway through the Ko'olau Mountains and stop at Nu'uanu Pali Lookout. The wind is intense and the views of the windward coast are incredible. Continue to Ho'omaluhia Botanical Garden, about 400 acres of landscaped grounds and a lake with a dramatic Ko'olau backdrop. Entry is free. Note: typically closed on Thursdays.
days.afternoon: Head into Kailua town for lunch at Kalapawai Market, then spend the afternoon at Kailua Beach Park, a long crescent of powdery sand and turquoise water. Before heading back, drive or walk south a short distance to Lanikai Beach, one of the most beautiful and least-crowded stretches of sand in the state. You can also rent kayaks nearby and paddle out toward the Mokulua Islands if conditions allow.
days.evening: Dinner at Buzz's Original Steakhouse, a family-owned Kailua landmark since 1962. Steaks, fresh seafood, and excellent mai tais. Open daily for lunch and dinner. The bar area is walk-in; call ahead for a table.
days.tip: Aim to reach Kailua Beach before late morning for easier parking. Lanikai has street parking only along Mokulua Drive with posted restrictions. For kayaking to the Mokulua Islands, check wind and surf conditions with the rental shop before heading out. Ho'omaluhia is closed Thursdays. Buzz's fills up fast on weekends, so call ahead if you want a table.

days.title: Rainforest Morning and Departure
days.morning: Manoa Falls Trail for a final rainforest hike. The parking lot (Paradise Park) opens at 8 AM with a $7 fee; free street parking is available on Waakaua Street if arriving early. About 1.6 miles round trip through lush jungle, taking 1 to 1.5 hours. Bring bug spray and shoes with traction, as the trail can be muddy. After the hike, grab brunch at Koko Head Cafe in Kaimuki (about 10 minutes away). Open Mon-Thu from 8 AM, Fri-Sun from 7 AM.
days.afternoon: Back to the hotel to pack and check out. Low-key afternoon in Waikiki if time allows. Head to HNL at least 2.5 hours before your domestic flight.
days.evening: Safe travels. A week well spent.
days.tip: Manoa Falls parking opens at 8 AM. Go early before the heat and crowds build. Plan to be at HNL 2.5 hours before departure. Return your rental car before the terminal.

beaches.title: Beach Guide
beaches.subtitle: Oahu's best shores: ranked, described, and honestly evaluated.

list.name: Waikiki Beach
list.desc: The iconic postcard beach. Calm, protected waters ideal for beginners and surf lessons. Very touristy, but undeniably beautiful at golden hour.

list.name: Kailua Beach Park
list.bestFor: Kayaking to Mokulua Islands, windsurfing, long walks
list.desc: A roughly 2-mile crescent of powdery white sand with turquoise water and consistent trade winds. One of the most beautiful beaches on Oahu, with kayak rentals nearby to reach the offshore Mokulua Islands in good conditions.

list.name: Lanikai Beach
list.desc: Powdery white sand and vivid blue water, especially at sunrise. No facilities, so bring everything you need. One of the most photographed beaches in the U.S. Street parking only along Mokulua Drive with posted restrictions.

list.name: Hanauma Bay
list.desc: A protected marine sanctuary in an ancient volcanic crater with excellent snorkeling and frequent turtle and reef-fish sightings. Closed Monday and Tuesday. Reservations required at web5.hnl.info, open two days ahead at 7 AM HST. Entry $25 per person plus $3 parking (cash only). Last entry 1:30 PM.

list.name: Waimea Bay
list.desc: A dramatic bay that changes with the seasons. Generally calm and swimmable in summer with the famous rock jump for thrill-seekers. In winter, huge swells and big-wave surf are best admired from shore.

list.name: Waimanalo Beach Park
list.desc: One of Oahu's longest and less-crowded beaches. Soft sand, clear water, and a stunning Ko'olau mountain backdrop. A favorite of locals with a more low-key vibe than Waikiki or many North Shore spots.

locations.label: Explore
locations.title: Locations and Attractions
locations.subtitle: Landmarks, shops, and experiences from your itinerary.

list.name: Diamond Head
list.desc: Iconic crater hike, about 1.6 miles round trip with paid entry ($5 per person plus $10 parking). Opens at 6 AM. Advance online reservations are required and sell out. The summit offers 360-degree views of Waikiki and southeast Oahu.
list.highlights: Hiking, panoramic views, military tunnels, sunrise

list.name: Kualoa Ranch
list.desc: Famous filming location for Jurassic Park, Lost, Godzilla, and dozens of other productions. Offers UTV Raptor tours, ziplines, and movie site tours through a lush valley. Book popular tours well in advance as they sell out.
list.highlights: UTV tours, ziplines, movie sites, valley views

list.name: Nu'uanu Pali Lookout
list.desc: Panoramic windward views from a cliffside viewpoint along the Pali Highway. Historic battle site of King Kamehameha's 1795 conquest of Oahu. Almost always extremely windy, so secure hats and bags. Small parking fee required.
list.highlights: Panoramic views, Ko'olau Mountains, windward coast, history

list.name: Ho'omaluhia Botanical Garden
list.desc: A roughly 400-acre botanical garden with a lake and a dramatic Ko'olau mountain backdrop. Free entry. One of the most photogenic spots on the island for quiet walks and photos. Typically closed Thursdays.
list.highlights: Free entry, Ko'olau backdrop, lake, scenic walks, photography

list.name: Ke Kai Catamaran
list.desc: Popular Waikiki sunset catamaran. Boards at 5 PM, departs at 5:30 PM, runs 90 minutes with complimentary drinks. Sea turtles and dolphins are frequently spotted. Check in at least 30 minutes early or you may lose your reservation. Operates daily.
list.highlights: Sunset sail, complimentary drinks, turtle and dolphin sightings, daily departures

list.name: Laniakea Beach
list.desc: Famous North Shore beach where Hawaiian green sea turtles often haul out on the sand to rest. Volunteer groups help educate visitors and keep everyone at a respectful distance. A memorable and reliable wildlife encounter.
list.highlights: Sea turtle sightings, volunteers, North Shore, wildlife

list.name: Manoa Falls Trail
list.desc: A lush rainforest trail to a waterfall just above Honolulu. About 1.6 miles round trip with no park admission fee. Cool, misty, and jungle-dense. Go early for cooler temps, bring bug spray, and expect some mud.
list.highlights: Rainforest hike, waterfall, no admission fee, uncrowded mornings

list.name: Royal Hawaiian Hotel
list.desc: The historic Pink Palace of the Pacific, opened in 1927. Spanish-Moorish architecture, lush courtyards, and prime oceanfront real estate on Waikiki Beach. Home to Azure Restaurant (Wednesday through Sunday evenings) and the legendary Mai Tai Bar, open daily.
list.highlights: Historic hotel, Azure restaurant, Mai Tai Bar, oceanfront, Waikiki landmark

list.name: Waikiki Beach Walk
list.desc: Open-air shopping and dining promenade just off the sand. Restaurants, boutiques, and frequent live entertainment within a few minutes walk of the beach.
list.highlights: Shopping, dining, live music, waterfront stroll

list.name: SALT at Our Kakaako
list.desc: Boutique shopping and dining hub in Kakaako. Local brands, galleries, and restaurants in a walkable urban district. Part of Honolulu's arts and culture neighborhood with mural-lined streets.

list.name: Ward Village
list.desc: A master-planned urban neighborhood in Kakaako developed by Howard Hughes Corporation. Opened in phases from 2016, it features luxury residences alongside curated retail, restaurants, and the Ward Entertainment Center. A walkable hub connecting Kakaako's arts district to the waterfront.
list.highlights: Boutiques, dining, Ward Entertainment Center, Kakaako arts district

list.name: Kakaako Waterfront Park
list.desc: A free, open oceanside park at the edge of Kakaako, with expansive lawn, a playground, and unobstructed views of the Pacific. Adjacent to Kewalo Basin, it's a relaxed spot to decompress after exploring the neighborhood's murals and galleries.
list.highlights: Free entry, ocean views, open lawn, waterfront, Kewalo Basin

list.name: Mokulua Islands
list.desc: Two small offshore islands (Moku Nui and Moku Iki) sitting just off Lanikai Beach. Both are state seabird sanctuaries. Moku Nui has a sandy beach and a short trail with sweeping views; Moku Iki is closed to visitors. Reach them by kayak from Kailua Beach Park or Lanikai in good conditions (typically 30–45 minutes each way).
list.highlights: Kayak destination, seabird sanctuary, sandy beach on Moku Nui, scenic trail

food.title: Food Guide
food.subtitle: From truck-side garlic shrimp to refined tasting menus. Oahu's culinary range is extraordinary.

localGems.name: Leonard's Bakery
localGems.whyGo: Since 1952. Fresh malasadas come out every few minutes. Get them plain or cinnamon-sugared and eat them hot. Cash and cards accepted.

localGems.name: Giovanni's Shrimp Truck
localGems.whyGo: The original North Shore shrimp truck since 1993. Garlic shrimp plates over rice. Haleiwa location at 66-472 Kamehameha Hwy (across from McDonald's), open daily 10:30 AM to 5 PM. Kahuku location 10:30 AM to 6:30 PM. Expect lines at peak lunch.

localGems.name: Aloh Health Bar and Cafe
localGems.whyGo: Plant-based cafe praised for its acai bowls, matcha lattes, avocado toast, juices, and coffee. A good morning stop before a beach day. Open daily 6:30 AM to 10 PM.

splurge.name: Merriman's Honolulu
splurge.whyGo: Farm-to-table Hawaii Regional Cuisine in a neighborhood bistro. Named Honolulu Magazine's Best Oahu Restaurant. Open daily 11 AM to 9 PM. Happy hour 3 to 5 PM. Reservations recommended. Free parking at Ward Village garages.

splurge.name: Roy's Hawaii Kai
splurge.whyGo: Roy Yamaguchi's original Hawaiian fusion restaurant, opened in 1988. Situated on Maunalua Bay in Hawaii Kai, about 20 minutes from Waikiki on the drive back from Waimanalo. Known for signature dishes like misoyaki butterfish and blackened ahi, with an extensive wine list. Open daily for dinner from 4:30 PM (to 9 PM, 9:30 PM Fri–Sat). Happy hour Mon–Thu 4:30–5:30 PM. Reservations recommended.

splurge.name: The Pig and The Lady
splurge.whyGo: Chef Andrew Le's celebrated modern Vietnamese restaurant, now in a bright new Kaimuki space (moved from Chinatown in 2025). The pho French dip, ahi tataki toast, and Le fried chicken wings are essential. Lunch Wednesday through Sunday, dinner Tuesday through Saturday. Closed Mondays. Reservations strongly recommended.

casual.name: Duke's Waikiki
casual.whyGo: The classic Waikiki beachfront dinner. Right on the sand with live music and a lively crowd. Go for huli huli chicken, fresh fish, and Hula Pie. Bar seating is often available without a reservation. Open daily.

casual.name: Kalapawai Market
casual.whyGo: Kailua's beloved neighborhood deli and general store. Excellent sandwiches, coffee, and grab-and-go items. Perfect before or after Kailua Beach.

casual.name: Liliha Bakery
casual.whyGo: Classic local bakery-diner famous for coco puffs, choux pastry filled with Chantilly cream. Counter seating captures a genuinely local feel. A good stop for late-night cravings or an early breakfast.

casual.name: Koko Head Cafe
casual.whyGo: Chef Lee Anne Wong's island-style brunch spot. Known for creative dishes like Breakfast Bibimbap and Cornflake French Toast. Open Monday through Thursday 8 AM to 2 PM, Friday through Sunday from 7 AM to 2 PM. Also open for dinner Friday through Monday, 5 to 9 PM.

casual.name: Haleiwa Joe's
casual.whyGo: Two locations: the North Shore spot overlooks Haleiwa harbor, and the Kaneohe location sits inside Haiku Gardens with koi ponds and Ko'olau mountain views. Fresh fish, hearty portions, relaxed vibe. North Shore opens at 4:30 PM daily. Kaneohe opens at 4 PM daily. No reservations taken at either location.

casual.name: Buzz's Original Steakhouse
casual.whyGo: A beloved Kailua institution since 1962, steps from Lanikai Beach. Family-owned for over six decades, known for prime steaks, fresh local seafood, and strong mai tais. The bar area is walk-in; tables fill quickly, especially on weekends. Open daily for lunch and dinner.

tips.title: Practical Tips
tips.subtitle: Essential knowledge for a seamless week on the island.

items.title: Rent a Car
items.body: Essential if you want to explore Oahu beyond Waikiki. Book at HNL airport from major agencies and budget roughly $50 to $80 per day depending on season and car type. Reserve ahead, as island inventory can be limited during holidays and school breaks.

items.title: Parking Strategy
items.body: Waikiki's municipal and public lots offer good value at $1 to $2 per hour, compared to $35 or more for hotel valet. On the North Shore, beach lots often fill by late morning. Arrive early and avoid leaving valuables in the car. Lanikai has limited street parking only along Mokulua Drive with signed restrictions.

items.title: Book These in Advance
items.body: Some experiences require early planning: Diamond Head hiking reservations (sell out, book online), Hanauma Bay snorkeling (reservations at web5.hnl.info open two days ahead at 7 AM HST and sell out within minutes), Kualoa Ranch tours, Ke Kai catamaran, and dinner reservations at Merriman's Honolulu, Roy's Hawaii Kai, and The Pig and The Lady.

items.title: Payment
items.body: Credit cards are accepted nearly everywhere on Oahu. Keep $40 to $60 in cash for food trucks, markets, and certain parking situations. Leonard's Bakery and many shave ice stands are cash-friendly, but most places also accept major cards.

items.title: Sun and Reef Safety
items.body: Oahu's UV index is strong year-round. Apply SPF 30 to 50 or higher every 2 hours, even on cloudy days. Hawaii requires reef-safe sunscreen with no oxybenzone or octinoxate, so look for mineral formulas with zinc oxide or titanium dioxide. Bring your preferred brand from home, as options can be pricier on-island.

items.title: Packing Essentials
items.body: Reef-safe sunscreen, a long-sleeve rash guard, water shoes for Hanauma Bay and rocky North Shore entries, a reusable water bottle, a light rain or wind layer for the windward side, basic snorkel gear if you have it, and a foldable tote or daypack for markets and beach days.
